batchClaims.claimTemplate:
For each language $\mathcal{L}$ in
\[
\{
  \langref{cSDD},
  \langref{d-SDNNF},
  \langref{DNNF},
  \langref{FBDD},
  \langref{nFBDD},
  \langref{nOBDD},
  \langref{SDD},
  \langref{SDNNF},
  \langref{uFBDD},
  \langref{uOBDD},
  \langref{OBDD},
  \langref{dec-DNNF},
  \langref{dec-SDNNF}
\},
\]
$\mathcal{L}$ supports Conjunction not in quasipolynomial time \citep{Darwiche_2002,TCZ_Forthcoming}.
batchClaims.descriptionTemplate:
In the language $\mathcal{L}$, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If $\mathcal{L}$ supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into $\mathcal{L}$ by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nedgeref{CNF}{$\mathcal{L}$} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since $\mathcal{L}$ does not support Bounded Conjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Conjunction is immediate.

batchClaims.claimTemplate:
For each language $\mathcal{L}$ in
\[
\{
  \langref{DNF},
  \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T},
  \langfam{SDD}{T},
  \langfam{SDNNF}{T},
  \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T},
  \langfam{cSDD}{T},
  \langfam{TDD}{T},
  \langfam{nOBDD}{<},
  \langfam{uOBDD}{<}
\},
\]
$\mathcal{L}$ supports Conjunction not in quasipolynomial time \citep{Darwiche_2002,TCZ_Forthcoming}.
batchClaims.descriptionTemplate: In the language $\mathcal{L}$, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If $\mathcal{L}$ supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into $\mathcal{L}$ by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nedgeref{CNF}{$\mathcal{L}$} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

batchClaims.claimTemplate:
For each language $\mathcal{L}$ such that \edgeref{OBDD}{$\mathcal{L}$} and \edgeref{$\mathcal{L}$}{DNNF},
$\mathcal{L}$ supports Bounded Conjunction not in quasipolynomial time \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.
batchClaims.descriptionTemplate:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \edgeref{OBDD}{$\mathcal{L}$} and \edgeref{$\mathcal{L}$}{DNNF}, $\mathcal{L}$ cannot support bounded conjunction.

batchClaims.claimTemplate:
For each language $\mathcal{L}$ such that \edgeref{OBDD}{$\mathcal{L}$} and \edgeref{$\mathcal{L}$}{SDNNF},
$\mathcal{L}$ supports Bounded Disjunction not in quasipolynomial time \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.
batchClaims.descriptionTemplate:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{SDNNF} computing $f\lor g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \edgeref{OBDD}{$\mathcal{L}$} and \edgeref{$\mathcal{L}$}{SDNNF}, $\mathcal{L}$ cannot support bounded disjunction in quasipolynomial time.

batchClaims.claimTemplate:
For each language $\mathcal{L}$ such that \edgeref{OBDD}{$\mathcal{L}$} and \edgeref{$\mathcal{L}$}{d-DNNF},
$\mathcal{L}$ supports Bounded Disjunction not in quasipolynomial time \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming,Bova_2016}.
batchClaims.descriptionTemplate:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{d-DNNF} computing $f\lor g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \edgeref{OBDD}{$\mathcal{L}$} and \edgeref{$\mathcal{L}$}{d-DNNF}, $\mathcal{L}$ cannot support bounded disjunction in quasipolynomial time.

Note that \citet[Theorem 9]{Bova_2016} proves the lower bound without explicitly noting the implication for Bounded Disjunction.

batchClaims.claimTemplate:
For each language $\mathcal{L}$ in
\[
\{
  \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T},
  \langfam{uOBDD}{<}
\},
\]
$\mathcal{L}$ supports Bounded Disjunction not in polynomial time (quasipolynomial unknown) \citep{Vinall-Smeeth_2024}.
batchClaims.descriptionTemplate: \citet[Theorem 5]{Vinall-Smeeth_2024} gives unambiguous \langref{DNF} representations of functions $f$ and $g$ such that, measured in the size $s$ of the corresponding source representations, $f \lor g$ requires \langref{d-SDNNF} size $s^{\widetilde{\Omega}(\log s)}$. The unambiguous \langref{DNF} representations can be transformed into $\mathcal{L}$ representations with only polynomial overhead, and every $\mathcal{L}$ representation can be transformed into \langref{d-SDNNF} with only polynomial overhead. Thus polynomial-time bounded disjunction for $\mathcal{L}$ would contradict the lower bound.

batchClaims.claimTemplate:
For each language $\mathcal{L}$ in
\[
\{
  \langref{cSDD},
  \langref{d-DNNF},
  \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T},
  \langref{dec-DNNF},
  \langref{dec-SDNNF},
  \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T},
  \langref{SDD},
  \langref{uFBDD},
  \langfam{uOBDD}{<}
\},
\]
$\mathcal{L}$ supports Singleton Forgetting not in polynomial time (quasipolynomial unknown) \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.
batchClaims.descriptionTemplate: If $\mathcal{L}$ supported Singleton Forgetting in polynomial time, then given two $\mathcal{L}$ circuits $f$ and $g$ we could form the decision $(x \land f) \lor (\neg x \land g)$ with only constant overhead and forget $x$, yielding $f \lor g$. This would give polynomial-time Bounded Disjunction for $\mathcal{L}$, contradicting the lower bound of \citet{Vinall-Smeeth_2024}.

batchClaims.claimTemplate:
For each language $\mathcal{L}$ such that \edgeref{DNF}{$\mathcal{L}$} and \edgeref{$\mathcal{L}$}{DNNF},
$\mathcal{L}$ supports Negation not in quasipolynomial time \citep{Bova_2014}.
batchClaims.descriptionTemplate: \citet[Lemma 5]{Bova_2014} gives 2-\langref{DNF} formulas $D$ whose negations require \langref{DNNF} size $2^{\Omega(\operatorname{size}(D))}$. If $\mathcal{L}$ supported Negation in quasipolynomial time, then \edgeref{DNF}{$\mathcal{L}$} and \edgeref{$\mathcal{L}$}{DNNF} would yield quasipolynomial-size \langref{DNNF} formulas for those negations, contradicting the lower bound.

batchClaims.claimTemplate:
For each language $\mathcal{L}$ in
\[
\{
  \langref{cSDD},
  \langref{d-SDNNF},
  \langref{FBDD},
  \langref{uOBDD},
  \langref{OBDD},
  \langref{dec-DNNF},
  \langref{dec-SDNNF}
\},
\]
$\mathcal{L}$ supports Disjunction not in quasipolynomial time \citep{Darwiche_2002,TCZ_Forthcoming}.
batchClaims.descriptionTemplate:
In the language $\mathcal{L}$, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If $\mathcal{L}$ supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into $\mathcal{L}$ by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nedgeref{DNF}{$\mathcal{L}$} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since $\mathcal{L}$ does not support Bounded Disjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Disjunction is immediate.

batchClaims.claimTemplate:
For each language $\mathcal{L}$ in
\[
\{
  \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T},
  \langfam{OBDD}{<},
  \langfam{SDD}{T},
  \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T},
  \langfam{cSDD}{T},
  \langfam{TDD}{T},
  \langfam{uOBDD}{<}
\},
\]
$\mathcal{L}$ supports Disjunction not in quasipolynomial time \citep{Darwiche_2002,TCZ_Forthcoming}.
batchClaims.descriptionTemplate: In the language $\mathcal{L}$, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If $\mathcal{L}$ supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into $\mathcal{L}$ by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nedgeref{DNF}{$\mathcal{L}$} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

batchClaims.claimTemplate:
For each language $\mathcal{L}$ in
\[
\{
  \langref{CNF},
  \langref{NNF}
\},
\]
$\mathcal{L}$ supports Forgetting not in polynomial time (quasipolynomial unknown) assuming P \neq NP \citep{Darwiche_2002}.
batchClaims.descriptionTemplate: If $\mathcal{L}$ supported FO, then the consistency of any \langref{CNF} representation $\Sigma$ could be tested in polynomial time: represent $\Sigma$ in $\mathcal{L}$, compute $\exists\text{Vars}(\Sigma).\Sigma$, and check the resulting constant representation. This would decide \langref{CNF} consistency in polynomial time, implying P = NP \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

batchClaims.claimTemplate:
For each language $\mathcal{L}$ in
\[
\{
  \langref{OBDD},
  \langfam{OBDD}{<},
  \langfam{SDD}{T},
  \langfam{cSDD}{T},
  \langref{TDD},
  \langfam{TDD}{T}
\},
\]
$\mathcal{L}$ supports Forgetting not in quasipolynomial time \citep{Darwiche_2002,VanDenBroeck_2015}.
batchClaims.descriptionTemplate: A quasipolynomial-time forgetting procedure for $\mathcal{L}$ would turn every \langref{DNF} representation into an equivalent $\mathcal{L}$ representation of quasipolynomial size in the input \langref{DNF} after introducing fresh selectors and forgetting them. This contradicts \nedgeref{DNF}{$\mathcal{L}$} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

languages.name: CNF
languages.definition: A \langref{CNF} is a conjunction of clauses $\bigwedge_{i=1}^mC_i$. A clause is a disjunction of literals $C_i=\bigvee_{j=1}^{k_i}l_j$ where a literal $l_j$ is a variable $x$ or its negation $\lnot x$. For example, $(x \lor y \lor \lnot z)\land (x\lor \lnot y)\land (z\lor w)$.

CO.description: Consistency checking for \langref{CNF} is NP-complete by the Cook-Levin theorem, the classic foundational result for SAT \citep{Cook_1971,Levin_1973}.

IM.description: To check whether a consistent term $\alpha$ implies a \langref{CNF} representation $\Sigma$, it suffices to verify that each clause of $\Sigma$ shares a literal with $\alpha$. This can be done in polynomial time \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

VA.description: A \langref{CNF} representation is valid iff every clause in it is valid (i.e., contains a complementary pair of literals). This can be checked in linear time \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

CT.description: \nopref{lang_27fffab2}{CT} assuming the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse, and \edgeref{lang_27fffab2}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time. If CNF supported Model Counting in polynomial time, then PI could too by compiling first. Therefore Model Counting is unsupported by CNF assuming the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse.
CT.assumption: the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse

EQ.description:
Equivalence implies Consistency \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, 
opref{lang_89649e36}{CO} assuming $P 
eq NP$. Therefore Equivalence is unsupported by CNF assuming $P 
eq NP$.

SE.description:
Sentential Entailment implies Equivalence \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, 
opref{lang_89649e36}{EQ} assuming $P 
eq NP$. Therefore Sentential Entailment is unsupported by CNF assuming $P 
eq NP$.

CE.description:
Clausal Entailment implies Consistency \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, 
opref{lang_89649e36}{CO} assuming $P 
eq NP$. Therefore Clausal Entailment is unsupported by CNF assuming $P 
eq NP$.

ME.description:
Model Enumeration implies Consistency \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, 
opref{lang_89649e36}{CO} assuming $P 
eq NP$. Therefore Model Enumeration is unsupported by CNF assuming $P 
eq NP$.

FO.description: If \langref{CNF} supported FO, then the consistency of any \langref{CNF} representation $\Sigma$ could be tested in polynomial time: represent $\Sigma$ in \langref{CNF}, compute $\exists\text{Vars}(\Sigma).\Sigma$, and check the resulting constant representation. This would decide \langref{CNF} consistency in polynomial time, implying P = NP \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

AND_C.description: Conjunction of \langref{CNF} representation is trivially another \langref{CNF} representation: simply take the union of all clauses \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

OR_BC.description: The disjunction of two \langref{CNF} representations $\alpha_1 \lor \alpha_2$ can be computed by taking all cross products of one clause from $\alpha_1$ and one from $\alpha_2$, removing redundant literals and valid clauses. The result is a \langref{CNF} of polynomial size \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

OR_C.description: It is straightforward to convert any term into an equivalent \langref{CNF} formula in polynomial time. Since specific \langref{DNF} formulas cannot be turned into equivalent \langref{CNF} formulas in polynomial space (\langref{DNF} $\not\leq$ \langref{CNF}), \langref{CNF} does not satisfy $\lor$C \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

CD.description: CD is trivially satisfied: replacing literals of the conditioning term by Boolean constants in a \langref{CNF} and removing valid clauses preserves the \langref{CNF} property \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

AND_BC.description: Here, \opref{lang_89649e36}{AND_C} in polynomial time. Since Conjunction implies Bounded Conjunction \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_89649e36} supports Bounded Conjunction.

SFO.description: Here, \opref{lang_89649e36}{CD} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_89649e36}{OR_BC} in polynomial time. Since Conditioning and Bounded Disjunction together imply Singleton Forgetting \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_89649e36} supports Singleton Forgetting.

NOT_C.description: Negation and Conjunction together imply Disjunction \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nopref{lang_89649e36}{OR_C}. Also, \opref{lang_89649e36}{AND_C} in polynomial time. Therefore Negation is unsupported by CNF.

languages.name: cSDD

CO.description: \edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CO} in polynomial time. Therefore cSDD supports Consistency in polynomial time.

EQ.description: \edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{EQ} in polynomial time assuming $P = \mathrm{BPP}$. Therefore cSDD supports Equivalence in polynomial time assuming $P = \mathrm{BPP}$.

CT.description: \edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{CT} in polynomial time. Therefore cSDD supports Model Counting in polynomial time.

VA.description: Here, \opref{lang_83e3b023}{CT} in polynomial time. Since Model Counting implies Validity \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_83e3b023} supports Validity.

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{CE} in polynomial time. Therefore cSDD supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

IM.description: \edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_1afefbe2} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_1afefbe2}{IM} in polynomial time. Therefore cSDD supports Implicant in polynomial time.

ME.description: \edgeref{lang_83e3b023}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{ME} in polynomial time. Therefore cSDD supports Model Enumeration in polynomial time.

SE.description:
opref{lang_b9d72a7c}{SE} assuming $P 
eq NP$, and \edgeref{lang_b9d72a7c}{lang_83e3b023} in polynomial time. If cSDD supported Sentential Entailment in polynomial time, then OBDD could too by compiling first. Therefore Sentential Entailment is unsupported by cSDD assuming $P 
eq NP$.

AND_C.description:
In the language \langref{cSDD}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{cSDD} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langref{cSDD} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nedgeref{CNF}{cSDD} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{cSDD} does not support Bounded Conjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Conjunction is immediate.

OR_C.description:
In the language \langref{cSDD}, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{cSDD} supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into \langref{cSDD} by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nedgeref{DNF}{cSDD} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{cSDD} does not support Bounded Disjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Disjunction is immediate.

AND_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \edgeref{OBDD}{cSDD} and \edgeref{cSDD}{DNNF}, \langref{cSDD} cannot support bounded conjunction.

OR_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{SDNNF} computing $f\lor g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \edgeref{OBDD}{cSDD} and \edgeref{cSDD}{SDNNF}, \langref{cSDD} cannot support bounded disjunction in quasipolynomial time.

SFO.description: If \langref{cSDD} supported Singleton Forgetting in polynomial time, then given two \langref{cSDD} circuits $f$ and $g$ we could form the decision $(x \land f) \lor (\neg x \land g)$ with only constant overhead and forget $x$, yielding $f \lor g$. This would give polynomial-time Bounded Disjunction for \langref{cSDD}, contradicting the lower bound of \citet{Vinall-Smeeth_2024}.

FO.description: Forgetting implies Singleton Forgetting \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nopref{lang_83e3b023}{SFO}. Therefore Forgetting is unsupported by cSDD.

NOT_C.description: Every \langref{cSDD} respects some vtree $T$. Apply the fixed-vtree negation procedure for \langfam{cSDD}{T}: \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015} proved that reduced \langfam{SDD}{T}{s} support negation in polynomial time by passing negation through the subs. The result is still a \langfam{cSDD}{T} formula, hence a \langref{cSDD} formula.

languages.definition: \langref{cSDD} $= \bigcup_T \langfam{cSDD}{T}$. \citep{VanDenBroeck_2015,Darwiche_2011}

languages.name: d-DNNF

EQ.description: \citet{Darwiche_Huang_2002} showed that equivalence of \langref{d-DNNF} representations can be tested in randomized polynomial time by reducing it to the problem of checking whether two \langref{d-DNNF} representations agree on a randomly chosen assignment. Under the derandomization hypothesis P = BPP, this yields a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm and hence \langref{d-DNNF} satisfies EQ.

CT.description: \langref{d-DNNF} satisfies CT: model counting can be performed in polynomial time by exploiting decomposability (multiplying counts at $\land$-nodes) and determinism (adding counts at $\lor$-nodes). This is the key tractability result motivating \langref{d-DNNF} as a compilation target \citep{Darwiche_2001b} \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

CO.description: \edgeref{lang_6c130090}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CO} in polynomial time. Therefore d-DNNF supports Consistency in polynomial time.

ME.description: Here, \opref{lang_6c130090}{CO} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_6c130090}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Consistency and Conditioning together imply Model Enumeration \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_6c130090} supports Model Enumeration.

CE.description: Here, \opref{lang_6c130090}{CO} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_6c130090}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Consistency and Conditioning together imply Clausal Entailment \citep{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2001a}, \langref{lang_6c130090} supports Clausal Entailment.

VA.description: Here, \opref{lang_6c130090}{CT} in polynomial time. Since Model Counting implies Validity \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_6c130090} supports Validity.

IM.description: Here, \opref{lang_6c130090}{VA} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_6c130090}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Validity and Conditioning together imply Implicant \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_6c130090} supports Implicant.

SE.description:
opref{lang_b9d72a7c}{SE} assuming $P 
eq NP$, and \edgeref{lang_b9d72a7c}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time. If d-DNNF supported Sentential Entailment in polynomial time, then OBDD could too by compiling first. Therefore Sentential Entailment is unsupported by d-DNNF assuming $P 
eq NP$.

AND_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \edgeref{OBDD}{d-DNNF} and \edgeref{d-DNNF}{DNNF}, \langref{d-DNNF} cannot support bounded conjunction.

AND_C.description: Conjunction implies Bounded Conjunction \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nopref{lang_6c130090}{AND_BC} in quasipolynomial time. Therefore Conjunction is unsupported by d-DNNF in quasipolynomial time.

OR_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{d-DNNF} computing $f\lor g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \edgeref{OBDD}{d-DNNF}, \langref{d-DNNF} cannot support bounded disjunction in quasipolynomial time.

Note that \citet[Theorem 9]{Bova_2016} proves the lower bound without explicitly noting the implication for Bounded Disjunction.

OR_C.description: Disjunction implies Bounded Disjunction \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nopref{lang_6c130090}{OR_BC} in quasipolynomial time. Therefore Disjunction is unsupported by d-DNNF in quasipolynomial time.

FO.description: Forgetting implies Singleton Forgetting \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nopref{lang_6c130090}{SFO}. Therefore Forgetting is unsupported by d-DNNF.

SFO.description: If \langref{d-DNNF} supported Singleton Forgetting in polynomial time, then given two \langref{d-DNNF} circuits $f$ and $g$ we could form the decision $(x \land f) \lor (\neg x \land g)$ with only constant overhead and forget $x$, yielding $f \lor g$. This would give polynomial-time Bounded Disjunction for \langref{d-DNNF}, contradicting the lower bound of \citet{Vinall-Smeeth_2024}.

CD.description: Conditioning preserves both decomposability and determinism. If $\alpha \land \beta \models \bot$ then $(\alpha|\gamma) \land (\beta|\gamma) \models \bot$ for any consistent term $\gamma$, since $((\alpha \land \beta)|\gamma) \land \gamma \models \bot$ and $\gamma$ is consistent \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

languages.definition: Boolean circuits with AND and OR gates; literals as inputs; and whose AND gates are \emph{decomposable} (children mention disjoint sets of variables) and whose OR gates are \emph{deterministic} (on any input, at most one child evaluates to $1$).\citep{Darwiche_2001b}

languages.fullName: Deterministic Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form
languages.name: d-SDNNF

CO.description: \edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CO} in polynomial time. Therefore d-SDNNF supports Consistency in polynomial time.

EQ.description: \edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{EQ} in polynomial time assuming $P = \mathrm{BPP}$. Therefore d-SDNNF supports Equivalence in polynomial time assuming $P = \mathrm{BPP}$.

CT.description: \edgeref{lang_ea9b5299}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{CT} in polynomial time. Therefore d-SDNNF supports Model Counting in polynomial time.

ME.description: Here, \opref{lang_ea9b5299}{CO} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_ea9b5299}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Consistency and Conditioning together imply Model Enumeration \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_ea9b5299} supports Model Enumeration.

CE.description: Here, \opref{lang_ea9b5299}{CO} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_ea9b5299}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Consistency and Conditioning together imply Clausal Entailment \citep{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2001a}, \langref{lang_ea9b5299} supports Clausal Entailment.

VA.description: Here, \opref{lang_ea9b5299}{CT} in polynomial time. Since Model Counting implies Validity \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_ea9b5299} supports Validity.

IM.description: Here, \opref{lang_ea9b5299}{VA} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_ea9b5299}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Validity and Conditioning together imply Implicant \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_ea9b5299} supports Implicant.

SE.description:
opref{lang_b9d72a7c}{SE} assuming $P 
eq NP$, and \edgeref{lang_b9d72a7c}{lang_ea9b5299} in polynomial time. If d-SDNNF supported Sentential Entailment in polynomial time, then OBDD could too by compiling first. Therefore Sentential Entailment is unsupported by d-SDNNF assuming $P 
eq NP$.

AND_C.description:
In the language \langref{d-SDNNF}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{d-SDNNF} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langref{d-SDNNF} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nedgeref{CNF}{d-SDNNF} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{d-SDNNF} does not support Bounded Conjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Conjunction is immediate.

OR_C.description:
In the language \langref{d-SDNNF}, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{d-SDNNF} supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into \langref{d-SDNNF} by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nedgeref{DNF}{d-SDNNF} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{d-SDNNF} does not support Bounded Disjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Disjunction is immediate.

AND_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \edgeref{OBDD}{d-SDNNF} and \edgeref{d-SDNNF}{DNNF}, \langref{d-SDNNF} cannot support bounded conjunction.

OR_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{SDNNF} computing $f\lor g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \edgeref{OBDD}{d-SDNNF} and \edgeref{d-SDNNF}{SDNNF}, \langref{d-SDNNF} cannot support bounded disjunction in quasipolynomial time.

CD.description: Conditioning preserves both structured decomposability and determinism. The vtree structure is maintained since $\text{Vars}(\Sigma|\gamma) \subseteq \text{Vars}(\Sigma)$, and mutual exclusivity of $\lor$-children is preserved under conditioning \citet[Proposition 4]{Pipatsrisawat_2008}.

FO.description: If \langref{d-SDNNF} satisfied FO, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled to an equivalent \langref{d-SDNNF} of size polynomial in the input \langref{DNF} (using fresh selector variables and applying FO). Since specific \langref{DNF} formulas require exponential-size \langref{d-SDNNF} representations (unconditionally), this is impossible. This unconditional result applies to the fixed-vtree case \citet[Proposition 4]{Pipatsrisawat_2008}.

NOT_C.description: \langref{d-SDNNF} is not closed under negation in polytime. \citet{Vinall-Smeeth_2024} proves that for every $n \in \mathbb{N}$, there exists a Boolean function $f$ with an equivalent \langref{d-SDNNF} of size $n$ such that any \langref{SDNNF} equivalent to $\neg f$ has size $n^{\Omega(\tilde{\log}\, n)}$. This super-polynomial lower bound holds for every vtree (not just a single tree), so the result applies to \langref{d-SDNNF} (not just \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}). Whether quasipolynomial closure holds remains open.

SFO.description: \citet[Corollary 1]{Vinall-Smeeth_2024} gives \langref{d-SDNNF} formulas of size $s$ whose existential quantification of one variable requires \langref{d-SDNNF} size $s^{\widetilde{\Omega}(\log s)}$. Hence \langref{d-SDNNF} does not support singleton forgetting in polynomial time.

languages.definition: \langref{d-SDNNF} $= \bigcup_T$ \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}. \citep{Pipatsrisawat_2008}

languages.fullName: Deterministic Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form (wrt a fixed vtree)
languages.name: d-SDNNF$_T$

CO.description: \edgeref{lang_91f812d0}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CO} in polynomial time. Therefore d-SDNNF$_T$ supports Consistency in polynomial time.

EQ.description: Here, \opref{lang_91f812d0}{SE} in polynomial time. Since Sentential Entailment implies Equivalence \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_91f812d0} supports Equivalence.

CT.description: \edgeref{lang_91f812d0}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{CT} in polynomial time. Therefore d-SDNNF$_T$ supports Model Counting in polynomial time.

ME.description: Here, \opref{lang_91f812d0}{CO} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_91f812d0}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Consistency and Conditioning together imply Model Enumeration \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_91f812d0} supports Model Enumeration.

CE.description: Here, \opref{lang_91f812d0}{CO} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_91f812d0}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Consistency and Conditioning together imply Clausal Entailment \citep{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2001a}, \langref{lang_91f812d0} supports Clausal Entailment.

VA.description: Here, \opref{lang_91f812d0}{CT} in polynomial time. Since Model Counting implies Validity \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_91f812d0} supports Validity.

SE.description: Here, \opref{lang_91f812d0}{CT} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_91f812d0}{AND_BC} in polynomial time. Since Model Counting and Bounded Conjunction together imply Sentential Entailment, \langref{lang_91f812d0} supports Sentential Entailment.

IM.description: Here, \opref{lang_91f812d0}{VA} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_91f812d0}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Validity and Conditioning together imply Implicant \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_91f812d0} supports Implicant.

OR_C.description: In the language \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nedgeref{DNF}{d-SDNNF_T} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

AND_C.description: In the language \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nedgeref{CNF}{d-SDNNF_T} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

FO.description: Forgetting implies Singleton Forgetting \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nopref{lang_91f812d0}{SFO}. Therefore Forgetting is unsupported by d-SDNNF$_T$.

OR_BC.description: \citet[Theorem 5]{Vinall-Smeeth_2024} gives unambiguous \langref{DNF} representations of functions $f$ and $g$ such that, measured in the size $s$ of the corresponding source representations, $f \lor g$ requires \langref{d-SDNNF} size $s^{\widetilde{\Omega}(\log s)}$. The unambiguous \langref{DNF} representations can be transformed into \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} representations with only polynomial overhead, and every \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} representation can be transformed into \langref{d-SDNNF} with only polynomial overhead. Thus polynomial-time bounded disjunction for \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} would contradict the lower bound.

SFO.description: If \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} supported Singleton Forgetting in polynomial time, then given two \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} circuits $f$ and $g$ we could form the decision $(x \land f) \lor (\neg x \land g)$ with only constant overhead and forget $x$, yielding $f \lor g$. This would give polynomial-time Bounded Disjunction for \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}, contradicting the lower bound of \citet{Vinall-Smeeth_2024}.

AND_BC.description: Bounded conjunction of two \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} formulas can be performed in polytime via the Apply algorithm, preserving both determinism and structured decomposability \citet[Proposition 4]{Pipatsrisawat_2008}.

CD.description: Conditioning preserves both decomposability and determinism, and it preserves the fixed vtree because restricting by a term only removes variables or replaces literals by constants. Hence the conditioned formula remains in \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} \citet[Proposition 4]{Pipatsrisawat_2008}.

NOT_C.description: Negation and Bounded Conjunction together imply Bounded Disjunction \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nopref{lang_91f812d0}{OR_BC}. Also, \opref{lang_91f812d0}{AND_BC} in polynomial time. Therefore Negation is unsupported by d-SDNNF$_T$.

languages.name: DNF
languages.definition:
A \langref{DNF} is a disjunction of terms $\bigvee_{i=1}^mT_i$. A term is a conjunction of literals $T_i=\bigwedge_{j=1}^{k_i}l_j$ where a literal $l_j$ is a variable $x$ or its negation $\lnot x$. For example, $(x \land y \land \lnot z)\lor (x\land \lnot y)\lor (z\land w)$.

Equivalently, a \langref{DNF} is a \langref{DNNF} of depth two with an output OR gate whose children are AND gates over literals.

VA.description: Validity checking for \langref{DNF} is coNP-complete. A \langref{DNF} is valid iff every assignment satisfies at least one term, which requires checking that term coverage is complete \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

CO.description: \edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CO} in polynomial time. Therefore DNF supports Consistency in polynomial time.

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CE} in polynomial time. Therefore DNF supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

ME.description: \edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{ME} in polynomial time. Therefore DNF supports Model Enumeration in polynomial time.

CT.description: \nopref{lang_6ae90adc}{CT} assuming the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse, and \edgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_4c204bf3} in polynomial time. If DNF supported Model Counting in polynomial time, then IP could too by compiling first. Therefore Model Counting is unsupported by DNF assuming the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse.
CT.assumption: the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse

EQ.description:
Equivalence implies Validity \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, 
opref{lang_4c204bf3}{VA} assuming $P 
eq NP$. Therefore Equivalence is unsupported by DNF assuming $P 
eq NP$.

SE.description:
Sentential Entailment implies Equivalence \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, 
opref{lang_4c204bf3}{EQ} assuming $P 
eq NP$. Therefore Sentential Entailment is unsupported by DNF assuming $P 
eq NP$.

IM.description:
Implicant implies Validity \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, 
opref{lang_4c204bf3}{VA} assuming $P 
eq NP$. Therefore Implicant is unsupported by DNF assuming $P 
eq NP$.

NOT_C.description: \citet[Lemma 5]{Bova_2014} gives 2-\langref{DNF} formulas $D$ whose negations require \langref{DNNF} size $2^{\Omega(\operatorname{size}(D))}$. If \langref{DNF} supported Negation in quasipolynomial time, then \edgeref{DNF}{DNNF} would yield quasipolynomial-size \langref{DNNF} formulas for those negations, contradicting the lower bound.

AND_C.description: In the language \langref{DNF}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{DNF} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langref{DNF} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nedgeref{CNF}{DNF} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

AND_BC.description: The conjunction of two \langref{DNF} representations $\alpha_1 \land \alpha_2$ can be computed by taking all cross products of one term from $\alpha_1$ and one from $\alpha_2$, removing redundant literals and inconsistent terms. The result is a \langref{DNF} of polynomial size \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

OR_C.description: Disjunction of \langref{DNF} representations is trivially another \langref{DNF} representation: simply take the union of all terms \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

FO.description: \langref{DNF} satisfies FO \citep{Lang_2003}. Forgetting variables from a \langref{DNF} representation can be done in polytime \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

OR_BC.description: Here, \opref{lang_4c204bf3}{OR_C} in polynomial time. Since Disjunction implies Bounded Disjunction \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_4c204bf3} supports Bounded Disjunction.

SFO.description: Here, \opref{lang_4c204bf3}{FO} in polynomial time. Since Forgetting implies Singleton Forgetting \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_4c204bf3} supports Singleton Forgetting.

CD.description: Here, \opref{lang_4c204bf3}{AND_BC} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_4c204bf3}{SFO} in polynomial time. Since Bounded Conjunction and Singleton Forgetting together imply Conditioning \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_4c204bf3} supports Conditioning.

languages.name: DNNF

CO.description: Consistency of a \langref{DNNF} representation can be decided by a bottom-up traversal. A literal or constant node is handled directly; an $\lor$-node is consistent iff at least one child is consistent; and an $\land$-node is consistent iff all children are consistent. The last test is sound because decomposability ensures that conjuncts mention disjoint variable sets, so independently satisfiable children have compatible models. Hence consistency is decidable in linear time in the circuit size.

ME.description: Here, \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CO} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Consistency and Conditioning together imply Model Enumeration \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_3bebcab7} supports Model Enumeration.

CE.description: Here, \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CO} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Consistency and Conditioning together imply Clausal Entailment \citep{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2001a}, \langref{lang_3bebcab7} supports Clausal Entailment.

VA.description:
opref{lang_4c204bf3}{VA} assuming $P 
eq NP$, and \edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time. If DNNF supported Validity in polynomial time, then DNF could too by compiling first. Therefore Validity is unsupported by DNNF assuming $P 
eq NP$.

SE.description:
opref{lang_b9d72a7c}{SE} assuming $P 
eq NP$, and \edgeref{lang_b9d72a7c}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time. If DNNF supported Sentential Entailment in polynomial time, then OBDD could too by compiling first. Therefore Sentential Entailment is unsupported by DNNF assuming $P 
eq NP$.

CT.description: \nopref{lang_6ae90adc}{CT} assuming the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse, and \edgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time. If DNNF supported Model Counting in polynomial time, then IP could too by compiling first. Therefore Model Counting is unsupported by DNNF assuming the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse.
CT.assumption: the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse

EQ.description:
Equivalence implies Validity \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, 
opref{lang_3bebcab7}{VA} assuming $P 
eq NP$. Therefore Equivalence is unsupported by DNNF assuming $P 
eq NP$.

IM.description:
Implicant implies Validity \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, 
opref{lang_3bebcab7}{VA} assuming $P 
eq NP$. Therefore Implicant is unsupported by DNNF assuming $P 
eq NP$.

AND_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \edgeref{OBDD}{DNNF}, \langref{DNNF} cannot support bounded conjunction.

AND_C.description:
In the language \langref{DNNF}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{DNNF} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langref{DNNF} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nedgeref{CNF}{DNNF} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{DNNF} does not support Bounded Conjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Conjunction is immediate.

NOT_C.description: \citet[Lemma 5]{Bova_2014} gives 2-\langref{DNF} formulas $D$ whose negations require \langref{DNNF} size $2^{\Omega(\operatorname{size}(D))}$. If \langref{DNNF} supported Negation in quasipolynomial time, then \edgeref{DNF}{DNNF} would yield quasipolynomial-size \langref{DNNF} formulas for those negations, contradicting the lower bound.

OR_C.description: Disjunction trivially preserves decomposability since decomposability only constrains $\land$-nodes. \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

CD.description: Conditioning preserves decomposability: if $\alpha$ and $\beta$ do not share variables, then $\alpha|\gamma$ and $\beta|\gamma$ do not share variables either, since $\text{Vars}(\alpha|\gamma) \subseteq \text{Vars}(\alpha)$ \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

FO.description: \langref{DNNF} satisfies FO: forgetting a set of variables in a \langref{DNNF} representation can be performed in polynomial time while preserving decomposability \citep{Darwiche_2001a} \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

OR_BC.description: Here, \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{OR_C} in polynomial time. Since Disjunction implies Bounded Disjunction \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_3bebcab7} supports Bounded Disjunction.

SFO.description: Here, \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{FO} in polynomial time. Since Forgetting implies Singleton Forgetting \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_3bebcab7} supports Singleton Forgetting.

languages.definition: Boolean circuits with AND and OR gates, literals as inputs, and whose AND gates are \emph{decomposable} (children mention disjoint sets of variables).\citep{Darwiche_2001a} Decomposability may be seen as the analogue in Boolean circuits to the notion of \emph{syntactic multilinearity} in arithmetic circuits.\citep{shpilka2010arithmetic}

languages.name: FBDD

CO.description: Consistency means existence of a satisfying root-to-1 path. For a general \langref{FBDD}, run graph reachability from the root to terminal 1 in linear time \citep{Gergov_1994,Bryant_1992}.

CT.description: Model counting is done by dynamic programming over the DAG representation of the free BDD, combining child counts at each decision node; total runtime is polynomial in graph size \citep{Gergov_1994,Bryant_1992}.

VA.description: Validity is equivalent to absence of any falsifying path. For a general \langref{FBDD}, check that terminal 0 is unreachable from the root (equivalently, all root-to-terminal paths evaluate to true); this is graph reachability in polynomial time \citep{Gergov_1994,Bryant_1992}.

EQ.description: \citep{Blum_1980} evaluate both free Boolean graphs under random assignments over a finite field; if values differ, the graphs are definitely distinct, and if values match, they are probably equivalent. Repetition decreases error probability exponentially. Under derandomization assumptions such as P = BPP, this also yields deterministic polynomial-time equivalence testing for \langref{FBDD}.

ME.description: Here, \opref{lang_684b1ca7}{CO} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_684b1ca7}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Consistency and Conditioning together imply Model Enumeration \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_684b1ca7} supports Model Enumeration.

CE.description: Here, \opref{lang_684b1ca7}{CO} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_684b1ca7}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Consistency and Conditioning together imply Clausal Entailment \citep{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2001a}, \langref{lang_684b1ca7} supports Clausal Entailment.

IM.description: Here, \opref{lang_684b1ca7}{VA} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_684b1ca7}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Validity and Conditioning together imply Implicant \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_684b1ca7} supports Implicant.

SE.description:
opref{lang_b9d72a7c}{SE} assuming $P 
eq NP$, and \edgeref{lang_b9d72a7c}{lang_684b1ca7} in polynomial time. If FBDD supported Sentential Entailment in polynomial time, then OBDD could too by compiling first. Therefore Sentential Entailment is unsupported by FBDD assuming $P 
eq NP$.

AND_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \edgeref{OBDD}{FBDD} and \edgeref{FBDD}{DNNF}, \langref{FBDD} cannot support bounded conjunction.

AND_C.description:
In the language \langref{FBDD}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{FBDD} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langref{FBDD} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nedgeref{CNF}{FBDD} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{FBDD} does not support Bounded Conjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Conjunction is immediate.

OR_C.description:
In the language \langref{FBDD}, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{FBDD} supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into \langref{FBDD} by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nedgeref{DNF}{FBDD} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{FBDD} does not support Bounded Disjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Disjunction is immediate.

OR_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{d-DNNF} computing $f\lor g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \edgeref{OBDD}{FBDD} and \edgeref{FBDD}{d-DNNF}, \langref{FBDD} cannot support bounded disjunction in quasipolynomial time.

Note that \citet[Theorem 9]{Bova_2016} proves the lower bound without explicitly noting the implication for Bounded Disjunction.

NOT_C.description: Negation of an \langref{FBDD} is achieved by switching the labels of the 0-sink and 1-sink. The decision property and read-once property are preserved \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

CD.description: Conditioning is easy on branching programs: replace each decision node labeled by a conditioned variable with the child consistent with the conditioning term. This preserves the free read-once property \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

SFO.description: Let $ROW_n$ and $COL_n$ test whether an $n \times n$ Boolean matrix has an all-1 row or an all-1 column. The selector construction $(s \land ROW_n) \lor (\neg s \land COL_n)$ has polynomial-size \langref{FBDD}{s}: the top variable $s$ chooses a rowwise or columnwise computation. Forgetting $s$ yields $ROW_n \lor COL_n$, whose \langref{FBDD} size is $\Omega(n^{-7/2}2^n)$ by \citet[Theorem 6.2.13]{Wegener_2000}.

FO.description: Forgetting implies Singleton Forgetting \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nopref{lang_684b1ca7}{SFO} in quasipolynomial time. Therefore Forgetting is unsupported by FBDD in quasipolynomial time.

languages.definition: Binary decision diagrams such that each root-to-sink path tests each variable at most once. Also known as read-once branching programs.\citep{Wegener_2000}

languages.name: IP

CT.assumption: the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse
CT.description: By duality with \langref{PI}: the negation of a formula in prime implicate form is in prime implicant (\langref{IP}) form. The number of models of $\neg\Sigma$ over $\text{Vars}(\Sigma)$ is $2^n$ minus the model count of $\Sigma$. Hence the \#P-hardness of counting for \langref{PI} transfers to \langref{IP} \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

IM.description: By definition, \langref{IP} is the set of prime implicants of a formula. A consistent term $\alpha$ implies $\Sigma$ iff $\alpha$ entails some prime implicant of $\Sigma$. This entailment check between terms runs in polynomial time \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

SE.description: \langref{IP} is a subset of \langref{DNF} and \langref{IP} satisfies IM, so checking sentential entailment reduces to checking implication between prime implicant forms. Two formulas are equivalent iff they share the same prime implicants (canonical form), giving SE \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

CO.description: \edgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CO} in polynomial time. Therefore IP supports Consistency in polynomial time.

ME.description: Here, \opref{lang_6ae90adc}{CO} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_6ae90adc}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Consistency and Conditioning together imply Model Enumeration \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_6ae90adc} supports Model Enumeration.

CE.description: Here, \opref{lang_6ae90adc}{CO} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_6ae90adc}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Consistency and Conditioning together imply Clausal Entailment \citep{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2001a}, \langref{lang_6ae90adc} supports Clausal Entailment.

EQ.description: Here, \opref{lang_6ae90adc}{SE} in polynomial time. Since Sentential Entailment implies Equivalence \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_6ae90adc} supports Equivalence.

VA.description: Here, \opref{lang_6ae90adc}{IM} in polynomial time. Since Implicant implies Validity \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_6ae90adc} supports Validity.

NOT_C.description: Dual of \langref{PI}: $\Sigma_n = \bigvee_{i=0}^{n-1}(x_{2i} \land x_{2i+1})$ is in \langref{IP} form but has $2^n$ prime implicates. Its negation has $2^n$ prime implicants. Hence \langref{IP} cannot satisfy $\neg$C \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

AND_BC.description: $\text{IP}(\alpha_1 \land \alpha_2) = \max(\{\beta_1 \land \beta_2 \mid \beta_1 \in \text{IP}(\alpha_1), \beta_2 \in \text{IP}(\alpha_2)\}, \models)$ (dual of Proposition 40, \citet{Marquis_2000}). The maximization removes subsuming terms, yielding an \langref{IP} form in polytime \citep{Darwiche_2002,Marquis_2000}.

AND_C.description: It is straightforward to convert a clause into an equivalent \langref{IP} formula in polynomial time. Since specific \langref{CNF} formulas cannot be turned into equivalent \langref{IP} formulas in polynomial space (\langref{CNF} $\not\leq$ \langref{IP}), \langref{IP} does not satisfy $\land$C \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

OR_BC.description: Combining prime implicants via disjunction requires distributing and re-minimizing terms, which can lead to an exponential explosion of new prime implicants. Let $\alpha_1 = \bigwedge_{i=1}^k p_i$ (one prime implicant) and $\alpha_2 = \bigvee_{i=1}^k \bigvee_{j=1}^m (\neg p_i \land q_{i,j})$ ($mk$ prime implicants). Then $\alpha_1 \lor \alpha_2$ has $(m+1)^k + mk$ prime implicants, an exponential blowup \citep{Chandra_1978} \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

CD.description: Given an \langref{IP} formula $\Sigma = \bigvee \gamma_i$, conditioning on $\gamma$ gives $\bigvee (\gamma_i|\gamma)$. Keeping only the logically weakest surviving terms yields a prime implicants formula equivalent to $\Sigma|\gamma$. This requires $O(n^2)$ entailment tests among terms, each polytime \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

SFO.description: The number of prime implicants of $\exists x.\Sigma$ can be exponentially greater than the number of prime implicants of $\Sigma$ \citep{Chandra_1978}. A specific construction with $mk+1$ prime implicants produces $(m+1)^k + mk$ prime implicants after forgetting one variable \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

OR_C.description: Disjunction implies Bounded Disjunction \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nopref{lang_6ae90adc}{OR_BC} in quasipolynomial time. Therefore Disjunction is unsupported by IP in quasipolynomial time.

FO.description: Forgetting implies Singleton Forgetting \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nopref{lang_6ae90adc}{SFO} in quasipolynomial time. Therefore Forgetting is unsupported by IP in quasipolynomial time.

languages.definition: A \langref{DNF} that is the disjunction of all minimal entailing terms (prime implicants). \citep{Quine_1952}

languages.name: MODS

CO.description: A representation $\Sigma$ is consistent iff it has at least one model. Given the set of models, we need only check nonemptiness of the set \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

CT.description: We need only take the cardinality of the set of models (which can be done in linear time) \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

VA.description: \edgeref{lang_e02902d0}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_89649e36}{VA} in polynomial time. Therefore MODS supports Validity in polynomial time.

IM.description: \edgeref{lang_e02902d0}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_89649e36}{IM} in polynomial time. Therefore MODS supports Implicant in polynomial time.

EQ.description: \edgeref{lang_e02902d0}{lang_d69995dd} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_d69995dd}{EQ} in polynomial time. Therefore MODS supports Equivalence in polynomial time.

SE.description: \edgeref{lang_e02902d0}{lang_6ae90adc} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6ae90adc}{SE} in polynomial time. Therefore MODS supports Sentential Entailment in polynomial time.

ME.description: \edgeref{lang_e02902d0}{lang_d69995dd} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_d69995dd}{ME} in polynomial time. Therefore MODS supports Model Enumeration in polynomial time.

CE.description: \edgeref{lang_e02902d0}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{CE} in polynomial time. Therefore MODS supports Clausal Entailment in polynomial time.

NOT_C.description: $\Sigma = \bigwedge_{i=1}^n x_i$ has only one model over $\{x_1,\ldots,x_n\}$ but its negation $\neg\Sigma$ has $2^n - 1$ models. Hence \langref{MODS} cannot satisfy $\neg$C \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

AND_BC.description: The bounded conjunction of two \langref{MODS} formulas (over the same variables) is simply taking the intersection of their model sets. If the variables differ, it is the intersection of their cylindrical extensions. The result is a \langref{MODS} representation in polytime \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

AND_C.description: Let $\Sigma_i = (x_{i,1} \lor x_{i,2})$ for $i = 1,\ldots,n$. Each $\Sigma_i$ has 3 models. But $\Sigma = \bigwedge_{i=1}^n \Sigma_i$ has $3^n$ models, so no polynomial-size \langref{MODS} representation exists \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

OR_BC.description: Let $\alpha_1 = \bigwedge_{i=1}^n x_i$ (1 model) and $\alpha_2 = y$ (1 model). Then $\alpha_1 \lor \alpha_2$ has $2^n + 1$ models over $\text{Vars}(\alpha_1) \cup \text{Vars}(\alpha_2)$. Since this already uses the disjunction of just two \langref{MODS} representations, \langref{MODS} does not satisfy $\lor$BC \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

FO.description: Given a \langref{MODS} formula $\Sigma$ and a set $X$, the \langref{MODS} representation of $\exists X.\Sigma$ is obtained by removing every leaf node labeled by a literal $x$ or $\neg x$ with $x \in X$ (Propositions 18 and 20, Lang et al. 2000). See also the polytime FO operation on \langref{DNNF} from \citep{Darwiche_2001a} \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

SFO.description: Here, \opref{lang_e02902d0}{FO} in polynomial time. Since Forgetting implies Singleton Forgetting \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_e02902d0} supports Singleton Forgetting.

CD.description: Here, \opref{lang_e02902d0}{AND_BC} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_e02902d0}{SFO} in polynomial time. Since Bounded Conjunction and Singleton Forgetting together imply Conditioning \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_e02902d0} supports Conditioning.

OR_C.description: Disjunction implies Bounded Disjunction \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nopref{lang_e02902d0}{OR_BC} in quasipolynomial time. Therefore Disjunction is unsupported by MODS in quasipolynomial time.

languages.definition:
The set of satisfying assignments (models) of the function.\citep{Darwiche_2002}

Equivalently, a suitably restricted \langref{DNF} whose terms are in one-to-one correspondence with the models.

languages.name: nFBDD

CO.description: \edgeref{lang_1df07cc3}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CO} in polynomial time. Therefore nFBDD supports Consistency in polynomial time.

ME.description: Here, \opref{lang_1df07cc3}{CO} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_1df07cc3}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Consistency and Conditioning together imply Model Enumeration \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_1df07cc3} supports Model Enumeration.

CE.description: Here, \opref{lang_1df07cc3}{CO} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_1df07cc3}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Consistency and Conditioning together imply Clausal Entailment \citep{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2001a}, \langref{lang_1df07cc3} supports Clausal Entailment.

VA.description:
opref{lang_4c204bf3}{VA} assuming $P 
eq NP$, and \edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time. If nFBDD supported Validity in polynomial time, then DNF could too by compiling first. Therefore Validity is unsupported by nFBDD assuming $P 
eq NP$.

SE.description:
opref{lang_b9d72a7c}{SE} assuming $P 
eq NP$, and \edgeref{lang_b9d72a7c}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time. If nFBDD supported Sentential Entailment in polynomial time, then OBDD could too by compiling first. Therefore Sentential Entailment is unsupported by nFBDD assuming $P 
eq NP$.

CT.description: \nopref{lang_6ae90adc}{CT} assuming the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse, and \edgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_1df07cc3} in polynomial time. If nFBDD supported Model Counting in polynomial time, then IP could too by compiling first. Therefore Model Counting is unsupported by nFBDD assuming the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse.
CT.assumption: the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse

EQ.description:
Equivalence implies Validity \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, 
opref{lang_1df07cc3}{VA} assuming $P 
eq NP$. Therefore Equivalence is unsupported by nFBDD assuming $P 
eq NP$.

IM.description:
Implicant implies Validity \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, 
opref{lang_1df07cc3}{VA} assuming $P 
eq NP$. Therefore Implicant is unsupported by nFBDD assuming $P 
eq NP$.

AND_C.description:
In the language \langref{nFBDD}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{nFBDD} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langref{nFBDD} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nedgeref{CNF}{nFBDD} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{nFBDD} does not support Bounded Conjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Conjunction is immediate.

NOT_C.description: \citet[Lemma 5]{Bova_2014} gives 2-\langref{DNF} formulas $D$ whose negations require \langref{DNNF} size $2^{\Omega(\operatorname{size}(D))}$. If \langref{nFBDD} supported Negation in quasipolynomial time, then \edgeref{DNF}{nFBDD} and \edgeref{nFBDD}{DNNF} would yield quasipolynomial-size \langref{DNNF} formulas for those negations, contradicting the lower bound.

AND_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \edgeref{OBDD}{nFBDD} and \edgeref{nFBDD}{DNNF}, \langref{nFBDD} cannot support bounded conjunction.

OR_C.description: Disjunction of \langref{nFBDD} formulas is obtained by adding a top-level nondeterministic $\lor$-node whose children are the input formulas. This preserves the free read-once property.

CD.description: Conditioning is easy on branching programs: replace each decision node labeled by a conditioned variable with the child consistent with the conditioning term. This preserves the free read-once property. \citet{Wegener_2000} gives this argument for EXOR-OBDDs in Theorem 10.5.1.

FO.description: To forget a variable, replace each decision node labeled by that variable with a nondeterministic $\lor$-node over its two children. Repeating this for all forgotten variables preserves the free read-once property and computes existential quantification.

OR_BC.description: Here, \opref{lang_1df07cc3}{OR_C} in polynomial time. Since Disjunction implies Bounded Disjunction \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_1df07cc3} supports Bounded Disjunction.

SFO.description: Here, \opref{lang_1df07cc3}{FO} in polynomial time. Since Forgetting implies Singleton Forgetting \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_1df07cc3} supports Singleton Forgetting.

languages.definition:
An \langref{nFBDD} is a \emph{nondeterministic} \langref{FBDD}, i.e., an \langref{FBDD} that is additionally allowed to contain \emph{guess} nodes.\citep{Wegener_2000}

Also known as a nondeterministic read-once branching program.

languages.name: NNF

CO.description:
opref{lang_89649e36}{CO} assuming $P 
eq NP$, and \edgeref{lang_89649e36}{lang_5bf00851} in polynomial time. If NNF supported Consistency in polynomial time, then CNF could too by compiling first. Therefore Consistency is unsupported by NNF assuming $P 
eq NP$.

VA.description:
opref{lang_4c204bf3}{VA} assuming $P 
eq NP$, and \edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_5bf00851} in polynomial time. If NNF supported Validity in polynomial time, then DNF could too by compiling first. Therefore Validity is unsupported by NNF assuming $P 
eq NP$.

SE.description:
opref{lang_b9d72a7c}{SE} assuming $P 
eq NP$, and \edgeref{lang_b9d72a7c}{lang_5bf00851} in polynomial time. If NNF supported Sentential Entailment in polynomial time, then OBDD could too by compiling first. Therefore Sentential Entailment is unsupported by NNF assuming $P 
eq NP$.

CT.description: \nopref{lang_6ae90adc}{CT} assuming the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse, and \edgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_5bf00851} in polynomial time. If NNF supported Model Counting in polynomial time, then IP could too by compiling first. Therefore Model Counting is unsupported by NNF assuming the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse.
CT.assumption: the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse

EQ.description:
Equivalence implies Consistency \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, 
opref{lang_5bf00851}{CO} assuming $P 
eq NP$. Therefore Equivalence is unsupported by NNF assuming $P 
eq NP$.

IM.description:
Implicant implies Validity \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, 
opref{lang_5bf00851}{VA} assuming $P 
eq NP$. Therefore Implicant is unsupported by NNF assuming $P 
eq NP$.

CE.description:
Clausal Entailment implies Consistency \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, 
opref{lang_5bf00851}{CO} assuming $P 
eq NP$. Therefore Clausal Entailment is unsupported by NNF assuming $P 
eq NP$.

ME.description:
Model Enumeration implies Consistency \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, 
opref{lang_5bf00851}{CO} assuming $P 
eq NP$. Therefore Model Enumeration is unsupported by NNF assuming $P 
eq NP$.

FO.description: If \langref{NNF} supported FO, then the consistency of any \langref{CNF} representation $\Sigma$ could be tested in polynomial time: represent $\Sigma$ in \langref{NNF}, compute $\exists\text{Vars}(\Sigma).\Sigma$, and check the resulting constant representation. This would decide \langref{CNF} consistency in polynomial time, implying P = NP \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

NOT_C.description: \langref{NNF} trivially satisfies $\neg$C since any propositional representation can be converted to \langref{NNF} by pushing negations to literals and this preserves the \langref{NNF} property \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

AND_C.description: Taking the conjunction of \langref{NNF} representations $\alpha_1 \land \alpha_2 \land \ldots$ trivially produces another \langref{NNF} representation, since \langref{NNF} places no restrictions on $\land$-nodes \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

CD.description: CD is trivially satisfied by \langref{NNF}: replacing literals of the conditioning term by Boolean constants preserves the \langref{NNF} property \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

AND_BC.description: Here, \opref{lang_5bf00851}{AND_C} in polynomial time. Since Conjunction implies Bounded Conjunction \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_5bf00851} supports Bounded Conjunction.

OR_C.description: Here, \opref{lang_5bf00851}{NOT_C} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_5bf00851}{AND_C} in polynomial time. Since Negation and Conjunction together imply Disjunction \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_5bf00851} supports Disjunction.

OR_BC.description: Here, \opref{lang_5bf00851}{NOT_C} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_5bf00851}{AND_BC} in polynomial time. Since Negation and Bounded Conjunction together imply Bounded Disjunction \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_5bf00851} supports Bounded Disjunction.

SFO.description: Here, \opref{lang_5bf00851}{CD} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_5bf00851}{OR_BC} in polynomial time. Since Conditioning and Bounded Disjunction together imply Singleton Forgetting \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_5bf00851} supports Singleton Forgetting.

languages.definition: A Boolean circuit with AND and OR gates with literals as inputs (i.e., negations may only appear at leaves). Also called \emph{De Morgan circuits}. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

languages.name: nOBDD

CO.description: \edgeref{lang_d24efe0e}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CO} in polynomial time. Therefore nOBDD supports Consistency in polynomial time.

ME.description: Here, \opref{lang_d24efe0e}{CO} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_d24efe0e}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Consistency and Conditioning together imply Model Enumeration \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_d24efe0e} supports Model Enumeration.

CE.description: Here, \opref{lang_d24efe0e}{CO} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_d24efe0e}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Consistency and Conditioning together imply Clausal Entailment \citep{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2001a}, \langref{lang_d24efe0e} supports Clausal Entailment.

VA.description:
opref{lang_4c204bf3}{VA} assuming $P 
eq NP$, and \edgeref{lang_4c204bf3}{lang_d24efe0e} in polynomial time. If nOBDD supported Validity in polynomial time, then DNF could too by compiling first. Therefore Validity is unsupported by nOBDD assuming $P 
eq NP$.

SE.description:
opref{lang_b9d72a7c}{SE} assuming $P 
eq NP$, and \edgeref{lang_b9d72a7c}{lang_d24efe0e} in polynomial time. If nOBDD supported Sentential Entailment in polynomial time, then OBDD could too by compiling first. Therefore Sentential Entailment is unsupported by nOBDD assuming $P 
eq NP$.

CT.description: \nopref{lang_6ae90adc}{CT} assuming the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse, and \edgeref{lang_6ae90adc}{lang_d24efe0e} in polynomial time. If nOBDD supported Model Counting in polynomial time, then IP could too by compiling first. Therefore Model Counting is unsupported by nOBDD assuming the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse.
CT.assumption: the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse

EQ.description:
Equivalence implies Validity \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, 
opref{lang_d24efe0e}{VA} assuming $P 
eq NP$. Therefore Equivalence is unsupported by nOBDD assuming $P 
eq NP$.

IM.description:
Implicant implies Validity \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, 
opref{lang_d24efe0e}{VA} assuming $P 
eq NP$. Therefore Implicant is unsupported by nOBDD assuming $P 
eq NP$.

AND_C.description:
In the language \langref{nOBDD}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{nOBDD} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langref{nOBDD} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nedgeref{CNF}{nOBDD} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{nOBDD} does not support Bounded Conjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Conjunction is immediate.

NOT_C.description: \citet[Lemma 5]{Bova_2014} gives 2-\langref{DNF} formulas $D$ whose negations require \langref{DNNF} size $2^{\Omega(\operatorname{size}(D))}$. If \langref{nOBDD} supported Negation in quasipolynomial time, then \edgeref{DNF}{nOBDD} and \edgeref{nOBDD}{DNNF} would yield quasipolynomial-size \langref{DNNF} formulas for those negations, contradicting the lower bound.

AND_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \edgeref{OBDD}{nOBDD} and \edgeref{nOBDD}{DNNF}, \langref{nOBDD} cannot support bounded conjunction.

OR_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{SDNNF} computing $f\lor g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \edgeref{OBDD}{nOBDD} and \edgeref{nOBDD}{SDNNF}, \langref{nOBDD} cannot support bounded disjunction in quasipolynomial time.

OR_C.description: Disjunction implies Bounded Disjunction \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nopref{lang_d24efe0e}{OR_BC} in quasipolynomial time. Therefore Disjunction is unsupported by nOBDD in quasipolynomial time.

CD.description: Conditioning is easy on branching programs: replace each decision node labeled by a conditioned variable with the child consistent with the conditioning term. This preserves the ordered decision-diagram property. \citet{Wegener_2000} gives this argument for EXOR-OBDDs in Theorem 10.5.1.

FO.description: To forget a variable, replace each decision node labeled by that variable with a nondeterministic $\lor$-node over its two children. Repeating this for all forgotten variables preserves the ordered decision-diagram property and computes existential quantification.

SFO.description: Here, \opref{lang_d24efe0e}{FO} in polynomial time. Since Forgetting implies Singleton Forgetting \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_d24efe0e} supports Singleton Forgetting.

languages.definition: \langref{nOBDD} $= \bigcup_{<}$ \langfam{nOBDD}{<}. \citep{Wegener_2000}

languages.fullName: Ordered Binary Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed variable order)
languages.name: OBDD$_<$

CO.description: Consistency can be checked in polynomial time by graph reachability: verify that there exists a path from the root to a terminal 1 node \citep{Bryant_1986}.

CT.description: Model counting on \langfam{OBDD}{<}{s} can be done in polynomial time by a single bottom-up pass \citep{Bryant_1986, Bryant_1992}.

EQ.description: After reducing both diagrams, \langfam{OBDD}{<}{s} are canonical: two functions are equivalent iff their reduced \langfam{OBDD}{<} diagrams are identical. This makes EQ a structural isomorphism check \citep{Bryant_1986, Bryant_1992, Meinel_Theobald_1998} (Theorem 8.11).

ME.description: Models of an \langfam{OBDD}{<} formula can be enumerated in time polynomial in the output size by traversing all root-to-1-sink paths. Each path corresponds to a (partial) assignment that can be expanded to full models.

VA.description: Validity can be checked in polynomial time by graph reachability: verify that no path from the root reaches the terminal 0 node, equivalently that every reachable terminal is 1 \citep{Bryant_1986}.

CE.description: Here, \opref{lang_d69995dd}{CO} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_d69995dd}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Consistency and Conditioning together imply Clausal Entailment \citep{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2001a}, \langref{lang_d69995dd} supports Clausal Entailment.

IM.description: Here, \opref{lang_d69995dd}{VA} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_d69995dd}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Validity and Conditioning together imply Implicant \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_d69995dd} supports Implicant.

SE.description: Here, \opref{lang_d69995dd}{NOT_C} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_d69995dd}{AND_BC} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_d69995dd}{CO} in polynomial time. Since Negation, Bounded Conjunction, and Consistency together imply Sentential Entailment \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_d69995dd} supports Sentential Entailment.

OR_C.description: In the language \langfam{OBDD}{<}, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If \langfam{OBDD}{<} supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into \langfam{OBDD}{<} by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nedgeref{DNF}{OBDD_<} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

FO.description: A quasipolynomial-time forgetting procedure for \langfam{OBDD}{<} would turn every \langref{DNF} representation into an equivalent \langfam{OBDD}{<} representation of quasipolynomial size in the input \langref{DNF} after introducing fresh selectors and forgetting them. This contradicts \nedgeref{DNF}{OBDD_<} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

NOT_C.description: Negation of an \langfam{OBDD}{<} is achieved by switching the labels of the 0-sink and 1-sink. The result is an \langfam{OBDD}{<} of identical size \citep{Bryant_1986}.

AND_BC.description: The Apply algorithm computes the conjunction of two \langfam{OBDD}{<} representations sharing the same variable ordering in polynomial time. The result is a reduced \langfam{OBDD}{<} \citep{Bryant_1986}.

OR_BC.description: The Apply algorithm computes the disjunction of two \langfam{OBDD}{<} representations sharing the same variable ordering in polynomial time. The result is a reduced \langfam{OBDD}{<} \citep{Bryant_1986}.

CD.description: Conditioning is easy on \langfam{OBDD}{<}: replace each decision node labeled by a conditioned variable with the child consistent with the conditioning term. The fixed order $<$ is preserved \citep{Bryant_1986}.

SFO.description: Here, \opref{lang_d69995dd}{CD} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_d69995dd}{OR_BC} in polynomial time. Since Conditioning and Bounded Disjunction together imply Singleton Forgetting \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_d69995dd} supports Singleton Forgetting.

AND_C.description: Negation and Conjunction together imply Disjunction \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nopref{lang_d69995dd}{OR_C} in quasipolynomial time. Also, \opref{lang_d69995dd}{NOT_C} in quasipolynomial time. Therefore Conjunction is unsupported by OBDD$_<$ in quasipolynomial time.

languages.name: PI

CE.description: By definition, \langref{PI} is the set of prime implicates of a formula. A clause $\gamma$ is entailed by $\Sigma$ iff $\gamma$ subsumes (is a weakening of) some prime implicate of $\Sigma$. This subsumption check runs in polynomial time \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

CT.assumption: the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse
CT.description: The model counting problem for monotone Krom formulas (conjunctions of clauses with at most two positive literals) is \#P-complete \citep{Roth_1996}. Such formulas can be converted to prime implicates form in polynomial time, so \langref{PI} does not satisfy CT \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

SE.description: \langref{PI} is a subset of \langref{CNF} and \langref{PI} satisfies CE, so checking $\Sigma_1 \models \Sigma_2$ for \langref{CNF} $\Sigma_2$ reduces to checking each clause of $\Sigma_2$ against the prime implicates of $\Sigma_1$. Two formulas are equivalent iff they share the same prime implicates (canonical form), giving SE \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

VA.description: \edgeref{lang_27fffab2}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_89649e36}{VA} in polynomial time. Therefore PI supports Validity in polynomial time.

IM.description: \edgeref{lang_27fffab2}{lang_89649e36} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_89649e36}{IM} in polynomial time. Therefore PI supports Implicant in polynomial time.

EQ.description: Here, \opref{lang_27fffab2}{SE} in polynomial time. Since Sentential Entailment implies Equivalence \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_27fffab2} supports Equivalence.

CO.description: Here, \opref{lang_27fffab2}{CE} in polynomial time. Since Clausal Entailment implies Consistency \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_27fffab2} supports Consistency.

ME.description: Here, \opref{lang_27fffab2}{CO} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_27fffab2}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Consistency and Conditioning together imply Model Enumeration \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_27fffab2} supports Model Enumeration.

NOT_C.description: The formula $\Sigma_n = \bigwedge_{i=0}^{n-1}(x_{2i} \lor x_{2i+1})$ is in \langref{PI} form but has $2^n$ prime implicants. Its negation has $2^n$ prime implicates. Hence \langref{PI} cannot satisfy $\neg$C \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

AND_BC.description: Combining prime implicates via conjunction requires distributing and re-minimizing clauses, which can lead to an exponential explosion of new prime implicates. Let $\alpha_1 = \bigvee_{i=1}^k p_i$ (one prime implicate) and $\alpha_2 = \bigwedge_{i=1}^k \bigwedge_{j=1}^m (\neg p_i \lor q_{i,j})$ ($mk$ prime implicates). Then $\alpha_1 \land \alpha_2$ has $(m+1)^k + mk$ prime implicates, an exponential blowup \citep{Chandra_1978} \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

OR_BC.description: $\text{PI}(\alpha_1 \lor \alpha_2) = \min(\{\beta_1 \lor \beta_2 \mid \beta_1 \in \text{PI}(\alpha_1), \beta_2 \in \text{PI}(\alpha_2)\}, \models)$ (Proposition 40, \citet{Marquis_2000}). The minimization removes subsumed clauses, yielding a \langref{PI} form in polytime \citep{Darwiche_2002,Marquis_2000}.

OR_C.description: It is straightforward to convert any term into an equivalent \langref{PI} formula in polynomial time. Since specific \langref{DNF} formulas cannot be turned into equivalent \langref{PI} formulas in polynomial space (\langref{DNF} $\not\leq$ \langref{PI}), \langref{PI} does not satisfy $\lor$C \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

CD.description: The prime implicates of $\Sigma \land \gamma$ can be computed in polytime when $\Sigma$ is in \langref{PI} form and $\gamma$ is a term (Proposition 36 and the following paragraph, \citet{Marquis_2000}). Since \langref{PI} satisfies FO, the prime implicates of $\Sigma|\gamma \equiv \exists\text{Vars}(\gamma).(\Sigma \land \gamma)$ follow in polytime \citep{Darwiche_2002,Marquis_2000}.

FO.description: The prime implicates of $\exists X.\Sigma$ are exactly the prime implicates of $\Sigma$ that do not contain any variable from $X$ (Proposition 55, \citet{Marquis_2000}). Hence FO for \langref{PI} simply filters out clauses containing forgotten variables \citep{Darwiche_2002,Marquis_2000}.

SFO.description: Here, \opref{lang_27fffab2}{FO} in polynomial time. Since Forgetting implies Singleton Forgetting \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_27fffab2} supports Singleton Forgetting.

AND_C.description: Conjunction implies Bounded Conjunction \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nopref{lang_27fffab2}{AND_BC} in quasipolynomial time. Therefore Conjunction is unsupported by PI in quasipolynomial time.

languages.definition: The subset of \langref{CNF} where formulas are the conjunction of all minimal entailed clauses (prime implicates). \citep{Quine_1952}.

languages.name: SDD

CO.description: \edgeref{lang_1afefbe2}{lang_3bebcab7} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_3bebcab7}{CO} in polynomial time. Therefore SDD supports Consistency in polynomial time.

EQ.description: \edgeref{lang_1afefbe2}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{EQ} in polynomial time assuming $P = \mathrm{BPP}$. Therefore SDD supports Equivalence in polynomial time assuming $P = \mathrm{BPP}$.

CT.description: \edgeref{lang_1afefbe2}{lang_6c130090} in polynomial time, and \opref{lang_6c130090}{CT} in polynomial time. Therefore SDD supports Model Counting in polynomial time.

ME.description: Here, \opref{lang_1afefbe2}{CO} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_1afefbe2}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Consistency and Conditioning together imply Model Enumeration \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_1afefbe2} supports Model Enumeration.

CE.description: Here, \opref{lang_1afefbe2}{CO} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_1afefbe2}{CD} in polynomial time. Since Consistency and Conditioning together imply Clausal Entailment \citep{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2001a}, \langref{lang_1afefbe2} supports Clausal Entailment.

VA.description: Here, \opref{lang_1afefbe2}{CT} in polynomial time. Since Model Counting implies Validity \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_1afefbe2} supports Validity.

IM.description: Here, \opref{lang_1afefbe2}{NOT_C} in polynomial time and \opref{lang_1afefbe2}{CE} in polynomial time. Since Negation and Clausal Entailment together imply Implicant \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{lang_1afefbe2} supports Implicant.

SE.description:
opref{lang_b9d72a7c}{SE} assuming $P 
eq NP$, and \edgeref{lang_b9d72a7c}{lang_1afefbe2} in polynomial time. If SDD supported Sentential Entailment in polynomial time, then OBDD could too by compiling first. Therefore Sentential Entailment is unsupported by SDD assuming $P 
eq NP$.

AND_C.description:
In the language \langref{SDD}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{SDD} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langref{SDD} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nedgeref{CNF}{SDD} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{SDD} does not support Bounded Conjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Conjunction is immediate.
