separatingFunctions.name: $\Sigma_n = \bigwedge_{i=0}^{n-1} \left( x_{2i} \vee x_{2i+1} \right)$
separatingFunctions.description: Requires that in each pair $(x_{2i}, x_{2i+1})$, at least one is true. Dual to the Pairwise \langref{DNF}.

separatingFunctions.name: $\Pi_n = \bigvee_{i=0}^{n-1} \left( x_{2i} \wedge x_{2i+1} \right)$
separatingFunctions.description: Requires that at least one pair $(x_{2i}, x_{2i+1})$ has both variables true. Dual to the Pairwise \langref{CNF}.

separatingFunctions.name: Parity function
separatingFunctions.description: $O_n = \bigoplus_{i=0}^{n-1} x_i$ has linear size \langfam{OBDD}{<} representations but exponential size \langref{CNF} and \langref{DNF} representations.

separatingFunctions.name: Hidden Weighted Bit
separatingFunctions.description: Let $w = \sum_{i=1}^n x_i$ be the Hamming weight of the input. Then $HWB_n(x_1, \ldots, x_n) = x_w$ (the $w$-th input bit). Has \langref{OBDD} size $2^{\Omega(n)}$ but polynomial \langref{SDD} and \langref{FBDD} size.

separatingFunctions.name: $OR_n = \bigvee_{i=1}^{n} x_i$
separatingFunctions.description: Separates languages that compactly represent disjunctions from those that cannot.

separatingFunctions.name: $\Sigma_n = \bigwedge_{i=1}^n (x_i \leftrightarrow y_i)$
separatingFunctions.description: Requires that each pair $(x_i, y_i)$ has equal truth values. An \langfam{OBDD}{<} with interleaved order $x_1 < y_1 < \cdots < x_n < y_n$ is polynomial, but a separated order $x_1 < \cdots < x_n < y_1 < \cdots < y_n$ forces exponential size.

separatingFunctions.name: Clique Detection Instances
separatingFunctions.description: Tests whether a directed graph on $n$ vertices (encoded as $I \in \{0,1\}^{n^2}$) contains a bidirectional clique of size $k$: $\Sigma_{n,k}(I) = 1 \iff I$ contains such a clique. Has $O(n^3)$ prime implicants but exponential \langref{FBDD} size for suitable $k$.

separatingFunctions.name: Sauerhoff function
separatingFunctions.description: Let $g_n: \{0,1\}^{2n} \to \{0,1\}$ be the function evaluating to 1 if and only if the sum of its inputs is divisible by 3. The Sauerhoff function $S_n: \{0,1\}^{n^2} \to \{0,1\}$ is defined on the $n \times n$ matrix $X = (x_{i,j})$ as $S_n(X) = R_n(X) \vee C_n(X)$, where $R_n, C_n : \{0,1\}^{n^2} \to \{0,1\}$ are defined by $R_n(X) = \bigplus_{i=1}^n g_n(x_{i1},x_{i2},...,x_{in}$ and $C_n(X) = R_n(X^T)$.

separatingFunctions.name: Jukna-Schnitger function
separatingFunctions.description: Tests triangle-freeness on graphs with respect to a fixed set of forbidden triangles $A \subset T_n$. \citet{Jukna_2002} show that for every $n$, there exists $A_n$ of size $O(n^2)$ such that any balanced rectangle cover of $JS_n^{A_n}$ has size $2^{\Omega(n^2)}$. Has \langref{PI} size $O(n^2)$ but \langref{DNNF} size $2^{\Omega(n^2)}$.

separatingFunctions.name: Generalized Hidden Weighted Bit
separatingFunctions.description: A variant of $HWB_n$ introduced by \citet{Bova_2016_a} on $2n+1$ variables $x_1, \ldots, x_n, y_0, \ldots, y_n$. Defined as $F_n = (P_0 \wedge \neg y_0) \vee (P_n \wedge y_n) \vee \bigvee_{i=1}^{n-1}((P_{i,0} \wedge \neg y_i) \vee (P_{i,1} \wedge y_i))$, where $P_j$ and $P_{i,b}$ are the partition functions from the HWB \langref{SDD} construction. The $y_i$ variables ensure all sub-\langref{SDD}s are pairwise non-equivalent, making the \langref{SDD} compressed. Has \langref{cSDD} size $O(n^3)$ but \langref{OBDD} size $2^{\Omega(n)}$, since conditioning on $y_i = 1$ recovers $HWB_n$.

separatingFunctions.name: Equality function
separatingFunctions.description: The equality function $EQ_n(x,y)$ is true iff $x_i = y_i$ for every $i \in \{1,\ldots,n\}$. It has linear-size \langref{OBDD} representations for interleaved variable orders, but can require exponential \langref{OBDD} size for separated orders.

separatingFunctions.name: Weighted sum function
separatingFunctions.description: The weighted sum function $WS_n$ is the standard \langref{OBDD}/\langref{FBDD} separation family whose output is selected by the Hamming-weight-weighted index of the input. It has polynomial-size \langfam{nOBDD}{<} representations under every variable order, but requires exponential \langref{FBDD} size.

definitions.title: Representation Language
definitions.statement: A \emph{representation language} $L$ is a set of Boolean formulas sharing a common syntactic structure, endowed with the standard Boolean semantics and a size measure $|\cdot|$, usually simply the count of all variables and connectives in the formula.

definitions.title: Language Family
Succinctness Between Languages
definitions.statement:
There are two intuitive definitions of succinctness: a \emph{time} requirement, i.e. that an efficient compilation algorithm exists between two languages; or the \emph{space} requirement, that formulas remain ``small'' in the new language. Since, for any computation, runtime is lower-bounded by output size, the latter definition is weaker.

We fix the convention that succinctness between representation languages is a size-only relation, with no requirement that a conversion algorithm exist \citep{Gogic_1995}. However, to the best of our knowledge, all known proofs of succinctness use a compilation algorithm to establish the size relationship; and all proofs that \emph{no} time-efficient compilation algorithm exists use space complexity bounds. An example to the contrary would be highly interesting.

Formally, let $L_1$ and $L_2$ be two languages. $L_1$ is \emph{at least as succinct as} $L_2$, written $L_1 \leq_p L_2$, if there exists a polynomial $p$ such that for every $\alpha \in L_2$ there exists an equivalent $\beta \in L_1$ with $|\beta| \leq p(|\alpha|)$.

We say $L_1$ \emph{polynomially compiles into} $L_2$ if there exists a polynomial-time algorithm that converts any $\alpha \in L_1$ into an equivalent $\beta \in L_2$.

Since all claims in this project work with either definition, we will sometimes use them as if they were interchangeable.

We additionally define quasipolynomial succinctness identically, except we require a quasipolynomial function $q(n) \in n^{log^{O(1)}(n)}$ satisfying the above properties and use the notation $\leq_q$.

definitions.title: Succinctness Between Families
definitions.statement:
Let $\mathcal{F}_1$ and $\mathcal{F}_2$ be language families. We write $\mathcal{F}_1 \leq_p \mathcal{F}_2$ if for every $L_2 \in \mathcal{F}_2$ there exists $L_1 \in \mathcal{F}_1$ such that $L_1 \leq_p L_2$.

Likewise, we say $\mathcal{F}_1$ polynomially compiles into $\mathcal{F}_2$ if for every $L_1 \in \mathcal{F}_1$ there exists $L_2 \in \mathcal{F}_2$ such that $L_1$ polynomially compiles into $L_2$.

The same convention applies between a language $L$ and a family $\mathcal{F}$ by identifying $L$ with the singleton family $\{L\}$.

definitions.title: Tractability of an Operation
definitions.statement: An operation $\mathcal{O}$ is \emph{tractable} on a representation language $L$ if there exists a polynomial-time (resp. quasipolynomial-time) algorithm that performs $\mathcal{O}$ on any input formula(s) in $L$. Following \citet{Selman_1996}, the point is to compile a propositional theory off-line into a language where target operations are tractable.

definitions.title: Query
definitions.statement:
A \emph{query} takes a formula $\alpha \in L$ (and possibly auxiliary inputs) and returns a Boolean answer or a numerical value without modifying $\alpha$.

The standard query operations in this project are \defref{query-co}{Consistency}, \defref{query-va}{Validity}, \defref{query-ce}{Clausal Entailment}, \defref{query-im}{Implicant}, \defref{query-eq}{Equivalence}, \defref{query-ct}{Model Counting}, \defref{query-me}{Model Enumeration}, and \defref{query-se}{Sentential Entailment}.

definitions.title: Transformation
definitions.statement:
A \emph{transformation} maps one or more formulas from $\alpha_1,\alpha_2,..., \alpha_m\ \in L$, where $m$ may be arbitrarily large, to a new formula $\alpha_{m+1} \in L$.

The standard transformations in this project are \defref{transformation-cd}{Conditioning}, \defref{transformation-fo}{Forgetting}, \defref{transformation-sfo}{Singleton Forgetting}, \defref{transformation-and-c}{Conjunction}, \defref{transformation-and-bc}{Bounded Conjunction}, \defref{transformation-or-c}{Disjunction}, \defref{transformation-or-bc}{Bounded Disjunction}, and \defref{transformation-not-c}{Negation}.

definitions.title: Consistency (CO)
definitions.statement: Given a representation $\alpha$, decide whether $\alpha$ is satisfiable, i.e., whether there exists an assignment $X$ such that $\alpha(X)=\top$.

definitions.title: Validity (VA)
definitions.statement: Given a representation $\alpha$, decide whether $\alpha$ is valid, i.e., $\alpha \equiv \top$.

definitions.title: Clausal Entailment (CE)
definitions.statement: Given a representation $\alpha$ and a clause $c$, decide whether $\alpha \models c$ (equivalently, whether $\alpha \wedge \neg c$ is unsatisfiable).

definitions.title: Implicant (IM)
definitions.statement: Given a representation $\alpha$ and a term $t$, decide whether $t \models \alpha$ (equivalently, whether $t \wedge \neg \alpha$ is unsatisfiable).

definitions.title: Equivalence (EQ)
definitions.statement: Given two representations $\alpha$ and $\beta$, decide whether $\alpha \equiv \beta$.

definitions.title: Model Counting (CT)
definitions.statement: Given a representation $\alpha$, compute the number of satisfying assignments: $|\{X:\alpha(X)=\top\}|$.

definitions.title: Model Enumeration (ME)
definitions.statement: Given a representation $\alpha$, enumerate all satisfying assignments, i.e., output $\{X:\alpha(X)=\top\}$.

definitions.title: Sentential Entailment (SE)
definitions.statement: Given representations $\alpha$ and $\beta$, decide whether $\alpha \models \beta$.

definitions.title: Conditioning (CD)
definitions.statement: Given a representation $\alpha$ and a consistent term $\gamma$, compute the conditioned representation $\alpha|_{\gamma}$ obtained by substituting the assignments in $\gamma$.

definitions.title: Forgetting (FO)
definitions.statement: Given a representation $\alpha$ and a set of variables $Y$, compute $\exists Y.\alpha$ (existentially quantifying the variables in $Y$).

definitions.title: Singleton Forgetting (SFO)
definitions.statement: Given a representation $\alpha$ and a variable $x$, compute $\exists x.\alpha = (\alpha|_{x=0}) \vee (\alpha|_{x=1})$.

definitions.title: Conjunction ($\wedge$C)
definitions.statement: Given representations $\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_m$, compute $\bigwedge_{i=1}^m \alpha_i$.

definitions.title: Bounded Conjunction ($\wedge$BC)
definitions.statement: Given two representations $\alpha_1,\alpha_2$, compute $\alpha_1 \wedge \alpha_2$.

definitions.title: Disjunction ($\vee$C)
definitions.statement: Given representations $\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_m$, compute $\bigvee_{i=1}^m \alpha_i$.

definitions.title: Bounded Disjunction ($\vee$BC)
definitions.statement: Given two representations $\alpha_1,\alpha_2$, compute $\alpha_1 \vee \alpha_2$.

definitions.title: Negation ($\neg$C)
definitions.statement: Given a representation $\alpha$, compute $\neg \alpha$.

operationLemmas.description: CO and CD imply ME: First test consistency (polytime via CO). If inconsistent, return the empty set. Otherwise, build a decision tree of models by iterating over variables $x_1, \ldots, x_n$ and using CD to test consistency of $\Sigma | \alpha \land x_i$ and $\Sigma | \alpha \land \neg x_i$ for each leaf term $\alpha$. Since one branch must succeed for consistent terms, the tree has $O(mn)$ tests where $m$ is the model count and $n$ is the variable count.
CO and CD imply CE: To test whether representation $\Sigma$ entails non-valid clause $\alpha$, i.e. $\Sigma \models \alpha$, it suffices to test whether $\Sigma | \neg\alpha$ is inconsistent.

VA and CD imply IM: To test whether consistent term $\alpha$ entails representation $\Sigma$, i.e. $\alpha \models \Sigma$, test whether $
eg\alpha \lor \Sigma$ is valid. This is equivalent to $
eg\alpha \lor (\Sigma | \alpha)$. Since $
eg\alpha$ and $\Sigma | \alpha$ share no variables, the disjunction is valid iff $
eg\alpha$ is valid or $\Sigma | \alpha$ is valid. Since $\alpha$ is consistent, $
eg\alpha$ cannot be valid. Thus we test validity of $\Sigma | \alpha$, constructible in polytime via CD and testable in polytime via VA.
EQ implies CO: $\mathsf{true}$ and $\mathsf{false}$ belong to every NNF subset. $\Sigma$ is inconsistent iff it is equivalent to $\mathsf{false}$.
EQ implies VA: $\mathsf{true}$ and $\mathsf{false}$ belong to every NNF subset. $\Sigma$ is valid iff it is equivalent to $\mathsf{true}$.
SE implies EQ: Sentences $\Sigma_1$ and $\Sigma_2$ are equivalent iff $\Sigma_1 \models \Sigma_2$ and $\Sigma_2 \models \Sigma_1$.
CT implies CO: Model counting (CT) requires enumerating all satisfying assignments. If a formula is inconsistent, it has zero models. Thus CT can determine CO by checking if the model count is zero.
CT implies VA: A formula is valid iff all $2^n$ assignments satisfy it. If we can count models in polytime (CT), we can check validity by testing if the count equals $2^n$.

IM implies VA: A formula $\Sigma$ is valid iff the complete term (all positive or all negative literals, i.e., any single model) implies $\Sigma$. Since IM tests whether a consistent term implies the representation, testing with a trivially consistent term gives validity.
CE implies CO: A formula $\Sigma$ is inconsistent iff $\Sigma \models \bot$, which is equivalent to $\Sigma$ entailing the empty clause. Clause entailment (CE) can test this directly.

ME implies CO: Model Enumeration requires outputting all models in time polynomial in the input size and number of models. In particular, it must determine whether any model exists (output the first model or halt with the empty set) in polynomial time, which is exactly the Consistency (CO) query.

Conjunction implies bounded conjunction: bounded conjunction is a special case of conjunction where one operand has bounded size, so any language closed under conjunction is also closed under bounded conjunction.

Disjunction implies bounded disjunction: bounded disjunction is a special case of disjunction where one operand has bounded size, so any language closed under disjunction is also closed under bounded disjunction.
Forgetting implies singleton forgetting: singleton forgetting eliminates a single variable, which is a special case of forgetting an arbitrary set of variables.

Conditioning and bounded disjunction imply singleton forgetting: to forget variable $x$ from $\Sigma$, compute $\Sigma|_{x=0}$ and $\Sigma|_{x=1}$ via conditioning, then combine via $\Sigma|_{x=0} \lor \Sigma|_{x=1}$ using bounded disjunction (one operand can serve as the bounded component).
Negation and conjunction imply disjunction (De Morgan): $\Sigma_1 \lor \Sigma_2 = \lnot(\lnot\Sigma_1 \land \lnot\Sigma_2)$. Negate both operands, conjoin, then negate the result.
Negation and disjunction imply conjunction (De Morgan): $\Sigma_1 \land \Sigma_2 = \lnot(\lnot\Sigma_1 \lor \lnot\Sigma_2)$. Negate both operands, disjoin, then negate the result.

Negation and bounded conjunction imply bounded disjunction (De Morgan): $\Sigma \lor \gamma = \lnot(\lnot\Sigma \land \lnot\gamma)$, where $\lnot\gamma$ (negation of a clause) is a term. Negate $\Sigma$, conjoin with the term $\lnot\gamma$ via bounded conjunction, then negate.

Negation and bounded disjunction imply bounded conjunction (De Morgan): $\Sigma \land \alpha = \lnot(\lnot\Sigma \lor \lnot\alpha)$, where $\lnot\alpha$ (negation of a term) is a clause. Negate $\Sigma$, disjoin with the clause $\lnot\alpha$ via bounded disjunction, then negate.
Negation and consistency imply validity: $\Sigma$ is valid iff $\lnot\Sigma$ is inconsistent. Negate via $\lnot$C, then test consistency via CO.
Negation and validity imply consistency: $\Sigma$ is consistent iff $\lnot\Sigma$ is not valid. Negate via $\lnot$C, then test validity via VA.

Negation and clausal entailment imply implicant testing: term $\alpha$ implies $\Sigma$ iff $\lnot\Sigma$ entails clause $\lnot\alpha$. Negate $\Sigma$ via $\lnot$C, then test clausal entailment of $\lnot\alpha$ (a clause) via CE.

Negation and implicant testing imply clausal entailment: $\Sigma$ entails clause $\gamma$ iff term $\lnot\gamma$ implies $\lnot\Sigma$. Negate $\Sigma$ via $\lnot$C, then test whether $\lnot\gamma$ (a term) implies $\lnot\Sigma$ via IM.

Negation, bounded conjunction, and consistency imply sentential entailment: $\Sigma_1 \models \Sigma_2$ iff $\Sigma_1 \land \lnot\Sigma_2$ is unsatisfiable. Negate $\Sigma_2$ via $\lnot$C, conjoin with $\Sigma_1$ via $\land$BC, then test consistency via CO.

Bounded conjunction and singleton forgetting imply conditioning: $\Sigma|\gamma$ for term $\gamma = l_1 \land \cdots \land l_k$ is computed iteratively by $\Sigma \leftarrow \exists x_i.(\Sigma \land l_i)$ for each literal $l_i$, using $\land$BC then SFO.

CT and $\land$BC imply SE: $\Sigma_1 \models \Sigma_2$ iff $|\text{models}(\Sigma_1 \land \Sigma_2)| = |\text{models}(\Sigma_1)|$. Compute the conjunction via $\land$BC, then count models of both the original formula and the conjunction via CT and compare.
